name: IJVM Memory Model
description: Test your knowledge of the IJVM virtual machine memory model, including memory areas, stacks, control registers, and access to local variables.

questions.question: The IJVM memory model is divided into four main areas. What are they?
options: L1 and L2 caches, Main Memory, and Magnetic Disk.
CPU Registers, Data Buses, Control Store, and the ALU.
Constant Pool, Local Variable Frame, Operand Stack, and Method Area.
Opcode Read Area, Exception Stack, Dynamic Heap Area, and Thread Set.

questions.question: In the IJVM, what is the main characteristic of the memory area known as the 'Constant Pool'?
options: It is dynamically allocated on each invocation of a new method and stores temporary variables.
It cannot be written by an IJVM program; it consists of constants, strings, and pointers to other areas. It is accessed by the CPP register.
It is treated exclusively as a byte array and contains the code of the running program.
It stores the operand stack used to perform mathematical operations such as addition and subtraction.

questions.question: The CPP, LV, and SP registers are memory pointers. How does the addressing of the PC register differ from these three in the IJVM model?
options: The PC points to primary cache memory, while the others point to static RAM.
The PC holds a byte address (offset by number of bytes), while CPP, LV, and SP are word pointers (offset by words).
The PC is an absolute 64-bit hardware pointer, while the others are virtual registers of the operating system.
There is no difference; all are incremented by bytes to avoid memory misalignment in the ALU.

questions.question: Whenever a method is called (invoked), a memory area is reserved for its local variables. Where and how is that method's 'Operand Stack' allocated?
options: It is allocated at the absolute end of RAM, growing from top to bottom.
It is grouped inside the Constant Pool and accessed only via direct addressing.
The operand stack space is allocated directly above the local variable frame and grows toward higher addresses.
It shares the exact same space as the local variables, overwriting them whenever an addition is performed.

questions.question: How are the LV and SP registers used in the context of the local variable frame and the stack?
options: Both always point to the start of the method area to fetch the next IJVM instruction.
LV points to the top of the operand stack, and SP points to the base (start) of the local variable frame.
LV points to the base of the local variables (and parameters) of the active procedure, while SP points to the top word of the operand stack.
They hold the exact values of mathematical variables, rather than acting as memory addressing pointers.

questions.question: What happens to the LV and SP registers and to memory allocation when a procedure 'A' calls a procedure 'B'?
options: The execution process fails if the variables exceed 256 bytes of absolute memory.
The memory for A's variables is permanently erased to save bus space.
B's local variables are stored at fixed addresses at the base of RAM (addresses 0 to 100).
B's variables are stored on the stack, on top of A's variables. LV is adjusted to point to B's new variables.

questions.question: Besides the local variable frame, what is the main use of the 'Operand Stack' pointed to by SP?
options: To store exclusively the return address of the IRETURN instruction.
To manage permanent hard disk storage.
To hold operands during the evaluation of an arithmetic expression (e.g., pop two words, add them, and push the result).
To keep the record of memory access security permissions of the JVM.

questions.question: In the described IJVM implementation, in which geometric direction of memory (addresses) do the stacks grow when new words are pushed?
options: They grow upward, toward higher memory addresses.
They grow downward, toward lower addresses (close to 0).
They grow sideways, invading the neighboring L1 cache blocks.
They do not grow, having a rigid and inflexible size locked by the compiler.

questions.question: What is the function of the memory region called the 'Method Area'?
options: To hold static and global variables freely accessible between different methods.
To contain the executable program (text), composed of a byte array that holds the instructions (opcodes) fetched by the PC.
To act as a temporary input/output (I/O) buffer for secure peripheral devices.
To keep constant pointers and fixed text strings that are never modified during execution.

questions.question: When a method returns (terminated using the IRETURN instruction), what happens to the memory that was being used by its local variable frame?
options: The operating system enters an interrupt to permanently write the final data to the hard disk.
That memory is backed up to the Constant Pool and the CPP pointer is updated.
The area is cleared by actively filling every byte with zeros and the MAR register is reset.
The memory used by the local variables is freed, and the stack returns to its previous state (restoring the caller's old PC and LV pointer values).
